card.indications: Treatment of major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, diabetic peripheral neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, and chronic musculoskeletal pain.
card.mechanism: Inhibits reuptake of serotonin and norepinephrine in the central nervous system, increasing neurotransmitter activity involved in mood and pain modulation.
card.dosing: Common adult dosing begins at 30 to 60 mg once daily depending on indication and tolerance. Some patients are titrated to 60 mg daily; higher doses may increase adverse effects without additional benefit for several indications.
card.routeAdministration: Swallow delayed-release capsules whole; do not crush or chew. Take consistently with or without food. Taper gradually rather than stopping abruptly.
card.contraindications: Concurrent or recent MAO-inhibitor therapy, uncontrolled narrow-angle glaucoma, and hypersensitivity. Avoid in substantial alcohol use, chronic liver disease, or severe renal impairment when labeling advises against use.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: increased suicidal thoughts and behavior in children, adolescents, and young adults. Other risks include serotonin syndrome, hepatotoxicity, severe hypertension, orthostatic hypotension, hyponatremia, abnormal bleeding, and withdrawal symptoms.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, dry mouth, sleepiness, insomnia, dizziness, constipation, decreased appetite, sweating, fatigue, and sexual dysfunction.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Suicidal behavior, serotonin syndrome, severe liver injury, hypertensive crisis, severe hyponatremia, seizures, angle-closure glaucoma, and major bleeding.
card.interactions: MAO inhibitors are contraindicated. Other serotonergic drugs increase serotonin-syndrome risk. NSAIDs, antiplatelets, and anticoagulants increase bleeding. CYP1A2 or CYP2D6 inhibitors may raise concentrations.
card.monitoring: Mood, suicide risk, anxiety, pain response, blood pressure, sodium in at-risk patients, liver symptoms or tests when indicated, weight, falls, and withdrawal symptoms during tapering.
card.patientTeaching: Benefits may take several weeks. Do not stop suddenly. Report suicidal thoughts, agitation, fever, muscle rigidity, severe nausea, jaundice, unusual bleeding, or eye pain. Avoid heavy alcohol use.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food if nausea occurs. Avoid excessive alcohol because of liver risk. Maintain hydration and fiber for dry mouth and constipation.

card.indications: Treatment of hypertension, symptomatic heart failure, and asymptomatic left-ventricular dysfunction to reduce progression to overt heart failure. IV enalaprilat is used when oral therapy is not feasible.
card.mechanism: Inhibits conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion and increasing bradykinin.
card.dosing: Adult oral hypertension therapy commonly begins at 5 mg once daily, with lower starting doses in patients receiving diuretics or with renal impairment. Heart-failure dosing often begins at 2.5 mg once or twice daily and is titrated. IV dosing is individualized.
card.routeAdministration: Check blood pressure, renal function, and potassium before and during therapy. May be taken with or without food. Avoid abrupt volume depletion. IV enalaprilat should be administered slowly with close blood-pressure monitoring.
card.contraindications: Pregnancy, history of ACE-inhibitor-related angioedema, hereditary or idiopathic angioedema, use with aliskiren in diabetes, and use within 36 hours of sacubitril/valsartan.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: fetal toxicity. Can cause life-threatening angioedema, severe hypotension, acute kidney injury, hyperkalemia, neutropenia, and cholestatic jaundice or hepatic failure.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dry cough, dizziness, headache, fatigue, hypotension, and increased potassium or creatinine.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Angioedema with airway obstruction, severe hypotension, renal failure, dangerous hyperkalemia, neutropenia, and hepatic failure.
card.interactions: Potassium supplements, salt substitutes, potassium-sparing diuretics, and trimethoprim increase hyperkalemia. NSAIDs may reduce effect and worsen renal function. Lithium levels may rise. Dual renin-angiotensin blockade increases harm.
card.monitoring: Blood pressure, renal function, potassium, cough, edema, weight, heart-failure symptoms, and signs of angioedema or infection.
card.patientTeaching: Avoid pregnancy and report it immediately. Seek emergency care for facial, tongue, or throat swelling. Rise slowly. Avoid potassium supplements and salt substitutes unless prescribed. Report reduced urine, weakness, or persistent cough.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Follow sodium guidance and avoid unapproved potassium-rich salt substitutes or supplements.

card.indications: Prevention and treatment of deep-vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, prevention of ischemic complications in acute coronary syndromes, and selected perioperative or medical-patient thromboprophylaxis.
card.mechanism: Enhances antithrombin activity, producing greater inhibition of factor Xa than factor IIa and reducing thrombin generation and clot propagation.
card.dosing: Dosing depends on indication, body weight, renal function, age, and pregnancy status. Prophylaxis commonly uses fixed once- or twice-daily dosing, while treatment commonly uses weight-based dosing such as 1 mg/kg every 12 hours. Severe renal impairment requires adjustment.
card.routeAdministration: Inject deep subcutaneously in the abdomen, alternate sites, do not expel the air bubble from prefilled syringes unless product instructions require otherwise, and do not rub afterward. Do not give IM. Certain acute coronary regimens include an IV bolus.
card.contraindications: Active major bleeding, history of immune-mediated heparin-induced thrombocytopenia within the past 100 days or with circulating antibodies, and hypersensitivity to enoxaparin, heparin, or pork products.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: spinal or epidural hematomas can cause permanent paralysis. Major or fatal bleeding, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, thrombocytopenia, and hyperkalemia may occur. Dose accumulation occurs in renal impairment.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Injection-site bruising or pain, minor bleeding, anemia, and mild thrombocytopenia.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Major hemorrhage, spinal or epidural hematoma, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia with thrombosis, retroperitoneal bleeding, severe thrombocytopenia, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: NSAIDs, antiplatelets, thrombolytics, other anticoagulants, SSRIs, and SNRIs increase bleeding risk. Neuraxial procedures require careful timing.
card.monitoring: CBC, hemoglobin, hematocrit, platelet count, renal function, bleeding, stool or urine blood, injection sites, and neurologic symptoms after spinal procedures. Anti-Xa levels are reserved for selected high-risk patients.
card.patientTeaching: Use exact injection technique and rotate abdominal sites. Report black stools, vomiting blood, severe headache, weakness, unusual bruising, or back pain, numbness, or bladder dysfunction after spinal procedures. Avoid NSAIDs unless approved.
card.nutrition: No routine vitamin K restriction is required. Avoid excessive alcohol and unapproved supplements that increase bleeding.

card.indications: First-line treatment of anaphylaxis, cardiac arrest and severe bradycardic or hypotensive emergencies, refractory shock, and selected airway or local vasoconstriction indications depending on formulation.
card.mechanism: Stimulates alpha1, beta1, and beta2 receptors, causing vasoconstriction, increased cardiac rate and contractility, bronchodilation, and reduced mediator release from mast cells.
card.dosing: Anaphylaxis is treated with IM epinephrine in the mid-outer thigh, commonly 0.3 to 0.5 mg of 1 mg/mL solution in adults, repeated as clinically needed. Cardiac-arrest dosing uses a different concentration and IV or IO route. Infusion dosing is titrated in critical care.
card.routeAdministration: For anaphylaxis, give IM in the thigh immediately; do not delay for antihistamines. Concentration errors can be fatal. Auto-injectors should be held in place for the product-specific time. IV bolus use requires emergency expertise.
card.contraindications: There is no absolute contraindication in life-threatening anaphylaxis or cardiac arrest. Use caution in cardiovascular disease, arrhythmias, hyperthyroidism, and with interacting sympathomimetics when circumstances permit.
card.warnings: Can cause severe hypertension, cerebral hemorrhage, pulmonary edema, myocardial ischemia, tachyarrhythmias, tissue necrosis with extravasation, and medication errors related to concentration or route.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Tremor, anxiety, palpitations, headache, dizziness, sweating, nausea, and transient tachycardia.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Ventricular dysrhythmias, myocardial infarction, severe hypertension, stroke, pulmonary edema, and tissue necrosis.
card.interactions: MAO inhibitors, tricyclic antidepressants, and other sympathomimetics enhance cardiovascular effects. Nonselective beta blockers may produce unopposed alpha vasoconstriction and reduce bronchodilation. Digoxin and some anesthetics increase arrhythmia risk.
card.monitoring: Airway, breathing, blood pressure, ECG, pulse, oxygenation, perfusion, recurrence of anaphylaxis, blood glucose and potassium during prolonged infusion, and IV site.
card.patientTeaching: Use the auto-injector immediately for suspected severe anaphylaxis, then call emergency services. Carry two devices, check expiration dates, store as directed, and seek emergency evaluation even if symptoms improve.
card.nutrition: No specific food restrictions. During acute treatment, nutrition is secondary to airway and hemodynamic stabilization.

card.indications: Treatment of susceptible respiratory, skin, pertussis, diphtheria, sexually transmitted, and other bacterial infections. Topical and ophthalmic products treat acne or susceptible eye infections.
card.mechanism: Binds to the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit and inhibits protein synthesis. It is usually bacteriostatic but may be bactericidal at higher concentrations.
card.dosing: Systemic dosing varies by formulation and infection. Adult oral regimens commonly total 1 to 2 g daily in divided doses, with higher doses for severe infection. IV dosing is weight and indication based. Formulations are not always interchangeable.
card.routeAdministration: Some oral forms are best taken on an empty stomach, while enteric-coated or delayed-release products may be taken as labeled. Shake suspension well. IV erythromycin requires dilution and slow infusion to reduce venous irritation and arrhythmia risk.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to macrolides, prior erythromycin-associated cholestatic jaundice or hepatic dysfunction, and dangerous interacting drugs such as certain ergot alkaloids, cisapride, or pimozide.
card.warnings: Can cause QT prolongation, torsades de pointes, severe hepatotoxicity, infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, C. difficile diarrhea, serious hypersensitivity, and severe drug-interaction toxicity.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, diarrhea, loss of appetite, and infusion-site pain.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Torsades de pointes, sudden cardiac death, cholestatic hepatitis, anaphylaxis, severe skin reactions, hearing loss at high doses, and C. difficile colitis.
card.interactions: Strong CYP3A inhibition can increase many drugs, including some statins, warfarin, carbamazepine, theophylline, colchicine, and certain calcium-channel blockers. Other QT-prolonging drugs increase arrhythmia risk.
card.monitoring: Infection response, cultures when available, bowel pattern, liver function, ECG and electrolytes in high-risk patients, hearing with prolonged high-dose therapy, and toxicity of interacting drugs.
card.patientTeaching: Complete therapy and report jaundice, severe abdominal pain, fainting, palpitations, hearing change, rash, or persistent watery diarrhea. Review all medications for interactions.
card.nutrition: Follow formulation-specific food instructions. Food may reduce stomach upset for some products but may alter absorption of others. Maintain hydration.
